mappings.rationaleAI: Ibsen revolutionized modern European drama by establishing prose realism in theatre (B.1.e) and creating middle-class tragedies dissecting bourgeois society (B.1.a.i, A.2). His transformative roles for actresses and status as the most performed playwright after Shakespeare mark him as central to Western theatre history (A.7, D.1).

Molière shaped European comic drama as France's greatest playwright, blending farce and social satire (B.1.a.i) with roots in commedia dell'arte improvisation (B.1.a.ii). His permanent stage under Louis XIV's patronage exemplifies court theatre (B.1.b) and actor-dominated production (B.1.d).

Elizabethan and Jacobean revenge tragedy from Kyd's Spanish Tragedy to Hamlet introduced psychological depth through soliloquy (A.1) and reflected transitions from medieval vengeance to Tudor legal systems, illustrating theatre as social expression (A.2, B.1.a.i, B.1.e).

Shakespeare's status as the preeminent playwright and debates over his universality illuminate fundamental questions about the nature of theatre as art (A.1). The role of theatrical tradition and institutional education in sustaining his canonical supremacy addresses acting and dramatic forms (A.7, B.1.a.i, D.1).

Shakespeare's archetypal tragedy combines poetic language with dramatic structure (B.1.a.i), featuring sonnets embedded in dialogue that demonstrate the interrelation of literature and theatre (A.8). Its enduring influence on theatre, opera, and popular culture illustrates dramatic performance's lasting power (A.7, D.1).

Aristophanes' Lysistrata exemplifies ancient Greek comedy as political commentary (A.2, B.1.a.i), with its bawdy humour and serious anti-war themes engaging audiences through the interrelation of performance and audience (A.4). Performed within Athenian civic theatrical culture (B.1.b), it is central to Western theatre history (D.1).

Shakespeare's Hamlet is a defining work of Western tragedy (B.1.a.i) whose soliloquies pioneered dramatic interiority and psychological complexity in acting (A.7), while the interplay of concealed thought and audience awareness exemplifies the interrelation of theatrical performance and audience (A.4).

Directly examines the origins of Western comedy in ancient Greek theatre (A.1, D.1), covering Aristophanes' politically satirical works and Menander's innovations in comic form (B.1.a.i). The combination of mass democratic audience appeal with political commentary illustrates theatre's social function (A.2) and civic theatrical tradition (B.1.b).

Shakespeare's eight Plantagenet history plays illustrate theatre as a vehicle for shaping historical memory and political narrative (A.2), combining dramatic literature with theatrical performance (A.8). The plays' Elizabethan style and use of chronicle sources exemplify period-specific theatrical production (B.1.e, D.1).

Christopher Marlowe pioneered blank verse tragedy on the English stage with Tamburlaine and Doctor Faustus (B.1.a.i), establishing dramatic conventions that shaped Elizabethan theatrical style (B.1.e). His innovations in tragic form mark a pivotal moment in Western theatre history (A.7, D.1).

Louis XIV's patronage of the arts, including supporting Molière's permanent stage and establishing academies for music and performance, made his court the definitive example of court theatre (B.1.b) in Western theatrical history (D.1).

Brecht is one of the greatest 20th-century playwrights; his development of epic theatre and alienation effects represents a major innovation in theatrical art (A), his plays like Mother Courage and The Caucasian Chalk Circle are landmark dramatic productions (B), and his theatrical theory reshaped modern theatre (C).
Lang's pioneering work in German Expressionist cinema and Hollywood noir constitutes a major chapter in the history of Western theatre and dramatic arts broadly, though his primary medium was film.
Henry IV Part 1 is a canonical work of Renaissance drama combining elements of tragedy and history play (B.1.a.i), central to the history of Western theatre (D.1).

King Lear is one of Shakespeare's greatest tragedies (B.1.a.i), central to the history of Western theatre (D.1), with its performance history including Tate's adaptation and later restoration of the original.
Blood Wedding, Yerma, and The House of Bernarda Alba are major works of dramatic tragedy (B.1.a.i) in the history of Western theatre (D.1).
Macbeth is one of Shakespeare's most psychologically intense tragedies (B.1.a.i), central to the English dramatic canon and the history of Western theatre (D.1).
